title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved multiple infrastructure projects including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopted a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for the Military Road project and consultant services for upcoming roadway projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Water and sewer connection agreement - Radkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for pavement settlement over sewer trenches, and upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street for manhole and concrete issues.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox repairs, curb line sweeping, and storm damage cleanup from a tornado near East Nick. A culvert failure on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch pipe. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed. The committee discussed salt supply, confirming 1,500 tons ordered with delivery scheduled before November.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration issues, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. The Kathleen lift station showed evidence of sump pump discharge into sanitary sewers. An INI study using In-Pipe Technologies will help identify infiltration sources. Water main flushing notices were being mailed with work starting the following week.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves 6,100 customers across Weston, Rothschild, Schofield, and Rib Mountain Metro. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and Wisconsin remains affordable for automobile ownership compared to neighboring states. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel was low bidder at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons ($126,900 total). Analysis showed this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to Wausau's costs, totaling about $130,000 savings on 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement penalties for illegal sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members distinguished between unknowing violations (first offense notification) and willful violations. After discussion, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day, with understanding that repeat offenders would face escalating consequences.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex builder needs temporary water/sewer connections via long lateral services until Radkey Avenue reconstruction extends mains through Outlot 1. Committee added language requiring reconstruction 'no later than 2029.' Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for utility work on Military Road between Wolfman and Helian Streets. Weston's share is $94,860 for water main relocation and sanitary sewer work. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented consultant proposals for construction inspection. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout: MSA at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue: Clark Dietz at $73,730 preferred over MSA at $70,400 due to full-time experienced staff versus intern. Committee approved MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 village share)
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelinek project and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to calculate cost impact of clear water infiltration for next meeting
Staff to provide inspector resumes for construction projects

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from chair and future items

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and elected unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as secretary unanimously.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the event, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year despite last year's $5,000 grant. A commissioner requested better tracking of hotel room usage from the hotels directly. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request that the organizers provide hotel usage data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing it as a unique venue. She noted meeting the candidates, including Anastasia who won the title. The candidates visited Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, marking the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates expressed appreciation for the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building has capacity for 50-60 officers (current staffing is 40 officers and 4 clerical staff) to accommodate future growth. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting it be located further north to allow for future building expansion. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code requirements. Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling practices. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT's was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion included potential future residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with Ehlers having prepared a presentation that may be shared with commissioners or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video conference. He requested the commission exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, urging staff to waive excessive landscaping standards similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get lost.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with twice-annual inspections beginning summer 2026
Staff to report back on inspection program findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg regarding wellhead protection compliance
Joint steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts with commissioners

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No substantive votes were taken beyond approving previous meeting minutes; the meeting focused on educating committee members about department functions, budgets, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call noting remote attendance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinance, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Aging Disability Resource Center overview presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting aye.

discussions.item: Committee structure and strategic plan overview
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction over Veterans Service, Social Services, Health Department, North Central Health Care, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. Chair Boots noted the county completed its comprehensive plan last session and will develop a new strategic plan by December 2025. He indicated future meetings will track progress against strategic plan objectives.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted the department's work on food/water safety with over 1,200 licenses, a new homeless system response position, and the Nurse Family Partnership program that just graduated its first cohort of two-year-olds. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness; Suginaka confirmed the department has emergency plans, trained staff, and recently conducted measles response exercises.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, describing the department's nearly 140 FTE staff handling child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting many participants had never had bank accounts or formal leases before. The department's $22 million budget includes approximately $7 million in tax levy.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his 3.0 FTE office had 8,149 veteran interactions in 2025, up significantly from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million in annual VA compensation flows to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts following 7 veteran suicides among the county's 30 total suicides in 2025. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained veterans can use any county's VSO and that state statute requires each county to have its own office.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with approximately 60 FTEs and 400 volunteers. He noted significant financial challenges with federal nutrition program funding flat since 2024 while food costs rose 40-45%. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. The ADRC went from 2011 to 2024 without requesting a levy increase. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meals program at the Hmong American Center.

Vice Chair Rosenberg caught an error in the announced next meeting date. Administrator Leonard had stated May 29th, but that is not a Wednesday. The correct date was confirmed as Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present full annual report with 2025 data to the full county board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for June 3rd at 3:00 PM
Committee members encouraged to email agenda item suggestions to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees and commissions with chair updates
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning whether the village has real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Motti supported the fees citing population growth requiring more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained that this ordinance allows reciprocal fire inspections between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. A food truck inspected by one participating department receives a sticker valid for all communities, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously with motion by Lope, second by Zagami.

Ordinance 26-012 amending chapter 82 regarding bicycles and e-bikes was discussed briefly, with trustees noting the difference between e-bikes and e-motos (which can go 52 mph on sidewalks). The ordinance passed unanimously with motion by Zagami, second by Clark.

Significant debate occurred over the policy language. Trustees Beck and others argued the policy should match what was voted on at the previous meeting - simply requiring 12-hour notice, camera on, and no participation in closed session - rather than including language about health, safety, and welfare determinations. Trustee Motti opposed getting involved in judging whose excuse is valid. An amendment to strike sentences requiring chairs to evaluate reasons was approved 5-2, with Hermling and Motti opposed. The final amended policy passed 5-2 with the same dissents.

Discussion-only item addressed concerns about the farmers market's relocation from Camp Phillips/Scoffield to Kennedy Park after the previous landowner developed the property. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors now can keep vehicles with them. Trustee Beck questioned costs but staff confirmed the market operates at a profit through vendor fees and grants. No action was taken; this was discussion only to gather feedback.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. Parks and recreation committee and finance/HR committee still have openings.

Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street was approved for secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained the licensing requirements for precious metals dealers and tracking stolen merchandise. Motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since building is still under construction. Passed unanimously as amended.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of Deon 52 Street spoke twice during public comments. First, he criticized hiring outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, and complained about lack of Kennedy Park project information in packets and administrator accountability. During the park fees public hearing, Pitel questioned whether the fee increase is justified without actual data on park maintenance costs.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance 26-008 adopted
Nuisance ordinance 26-011 adopted
E-bike ordinance 26-012 adopted
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation fund solution adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove chair evaluation of reasons
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved pending background check and occupancy
Comprehensive Plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit study back to board or joint meeting
Staff to prepare intergovernmental agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Committee and commission applications to be included in board packets going forward
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution modifying landfill tipping fees to provide relief for residents affected by recent severe weather and tornado damage in the Ringle area. The meeting included special public comment from disaster-affected residents requesting fee waivers.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Motion to suspend rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, providing leadership development opportunities, and accommodating supervisor preferences. Over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, passed but was not unanimous. Supervisor G noted he was marked absent but wanted to register as voting 'I' on the matter.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur for advocating for the county's youngest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were used for voting, and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to serve on the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fees modification for disaster relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher explained the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for debris from the severe weather emergency. The state is waiving $13.10 per ton, Ringle is waiving $2.33 per ton, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (from $65 standard rate) to cover costs. About 400 tons of debris has been received so far. Supervisor Rosenberg requested special dispensation for public comment, which was granted without objection. Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck spoke, requesting Marathon County wave fees entirely and extend the relief period to six months given many insurance adjusters haven't yet assessed damage. After extensive discussion about policy implications and FEMA reimbursement concerns, the board unanimously approved suspending rules to consider the resolution, then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave fees entirely, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their fees, and requested a six-month extension. She emphasized that private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in hauling and cleanup and asked for comparable county contribution. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 homes affected, noting many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived and requested fee relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to serve as at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase (not unanimous)
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment (not unanimous)
Resolution 26-26 approved unanimously to reduce landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94 per ton for disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with community foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop a clear policy within six months on handling similar disaster situations in the future

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Parks, Recreation and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Staff presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature and potential loss of natural areas. Garrett Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make these conforming uses. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the tornado. Administrator Lance explained this was triggered by the governor's emergency declaration. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about fairness to those affected by non-declared disasters. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees for septic systems ($160), not other fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though the vote was not unanimous.

Dave Hagenbusher presented on Solid Waste operations including the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032, and capital projects. Tom Lovelin presented on Parks, Recreation and Forestry, noting his upcoming retirement June 1st after 30 years. Supervisor Sefeld praised the department and acknowledged Tom's service. Lori Mskimmons began the CPZ presentation before the transcript ended.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board (reconnect fees only for April 17, 2026 tornado victims)
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to county board
Schedule summer educational meeting on conservation topics
Track costs and requests related to fee waiver program for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved signage for Yellow Banks kayak launches and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the committee chair can be present. The meeting also included updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and discussion of graffiti removal challenges on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs discussion and action

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. No discussion was held and the motion carried unanimously.

Director Sean Oster presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from its inception through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about fundraising timelines, with staff explaining there is no firm deadline but a 10-year goal for completion. The Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for $117,000 in lights for the upper field and have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation. Staff noted the friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. A committee member emphasized that the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers and shouldn't be pressured to release numbers prematurely.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos showing the location of trees removed from the river and documentation of wood mats installed over gas pipelines to protect them during logging operations. The mats are approximately 8x8 timbers with notches and lifting rings. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR Pipeline) inspected the work. Some extra mats remain that may be used on another trail project.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy (6504 [inaudible]) spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches, noting their best tool is a sandblaster on a pressure washer which cannot access the trail. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and potential volunteer workdays. Laro suggested community volunteers would likely help given how much people love the trail. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan Mc from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version 7 of proposed signage for kayak launches along the river. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks) plus locations in Schofield. Marathon County Parks Foundation is funding the signs through state grants. The committee approved installation of the signs with a motion and second, motion carried unanimously. Staff noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement.

publicComment: Two residents provided public comment. Andy from 6504 [inaudible] Street spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools and suggesting rivers and lakes are appropriate for dogs instead. Jim Pensan (appearing online as 'Vern M') from 5002 [inaudible] Street Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned the Kennedy Park funding report showing exactly $8,700 in receipts and expenditures as 'too coincidental,' asked about the $10,000 grant and Glow event funds, and accused the Friends of Kennedy Park of being 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Approved installation of Yellow Banks kayak launch signs at village locations (to go to village board)
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal and prevention options for Oaklair River Trail
Staff to investigate food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds for future discussion
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m.
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes, acknowledged budget and financial reports, and received an educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's office operations including elections, licensing, and tax collection. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about final numbers for TIFF closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, noting the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Staff confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt as planned, with bonds due October 1st.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIFF reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of the clerk's office operations, staffing (currently 2 full-time and 1 part-time employee, down from 4 full-time previously), elections administration, licensing, and tax collection. She noted 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's office counter. Discussion included pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs, and concerns about the $30,000 annual cost of the quarterly newsletter 'The Wire.' Committee members discussed potentially revisiting digital distribution options.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with full vendor capacity including new food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft people. Produce vendors with greenhouses are participating, with more expected mid to end of June. Committee members raised concerns about parking congestion on Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson, with the east side signed no parking.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging members to reach out to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIFF bonds, anticipated this summer with bonds due October 1st
Continue monitoring parking situation at farmers market location
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots on Finance and HR Committee and other committees
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th and recommended approval of amendments to nuisance ordinances, an e-bike ordinance prohibiting e-motos on roadways, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both for further information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location at a park, stating vehicles parking on grass violates village code and ADA-compliant handicap spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass. He questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks and expressed concern that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.

Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after property sale and permit submission for repairs. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day) and 410 traffic stops with 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with new officers starting in May and January. Two SROs are assigned to zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 made by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet). Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred about e-motos, which Chief Hunt recommended prohibiting on roadways and sidewalks due to safety concerns, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee debated whether to include electric scooters in restrictions. Chief Hunt suggested officers could use discretion for children's play scooters. Motion to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property made by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Trustee Brent Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 5-hour windows). Michael from BPW explained daytime restrictions help plowing operations but acknowledged only 6-8 problem areas exist. Chief Hunt preferred overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. Committee decided to defer the item until July to allow Michael to identify specific problem areas for targeted signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk to connect to DC Everest parking lot they use on Sundays. Committee discussed costs ($2,000-$3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-$30,000 for lighted system). Chief Hunt had no safety concerns as no incidents have occurred. Motion to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs made by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a regional partnership with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer for mutual food truck fire inspections. A sticker system allows vendors inspected by any participating department to operate in all jurisdictions. A recent inspection failed a vendor due to improper LP piping. Motion to approve Ordinance 26-8 made by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Planning staff presented three address corrections for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments with eight separate addresses for one building. Motion to recommend approval made and seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization for raze order on an abandoned structure (abandoned since 2010-2011). A previous fire department training burn was cancelled due to unsafe conditions. The owners are current on taxes and prefer demolition. Costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion to recommend board authorization made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location, questioning parking on grass as a code violation, ADA compliance for handicap spaces, the need for food truck fire inspections, and lack of progress on long-standing code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of e-bike ordinance with e-moto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Michael to identify specific problem areas for overnight parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost sharing; if they agree to pay, recommend board approval
Recommend approval of Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend approval of address corrections for E911 compliance
Recommend board authorization to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
